previewTitle.value: Textures of artistic activism - Rolex mentoring programme

subtitle.value: Carrie Mae Weems & Camila Rodríguez Triana

text.value: <b>A shared interest in the politics of racial identity created a deep connection between Carrie Mae Weems and her protégée Camila Rodríguez Triana.<b/>

The warmth between artist Carrie Mae Weems and her protégée Camila Rodríguez Triana shines across two continents. Part of Triana’s appeal as a protégée for Weems was that she came from a different country and another world, yet their work is bound by ties of similarity. Both explore the impact of colonialism, displacement, identity and belonging, both have a strand of memory and remembrance in the pieces they make. Those affinities made Weems believe that she could gently guide the younger artist.

“Having a mentor is a gift,” Weems says. “Having somebody who deeply cares about you, who cares about the meaning of work and who sees its promise, but also sees where it needs to go, and who is willing to work with you to achieve that potential is a gift. On meeting Camila, I knew that I could be a good mentor for her, I knew that I could push her to see things in ways others might not.”

“The word that defines Carrie Mae for me is generosity,” says Triana. “You have the light, and you share it with other people. She has shared so much with me.”

For Weems, the younger artist has been equally giving. “She’s not warm or fuzzy, but she is deeply compassionate. I saw in her a tenacity along with an abiding seriousness to explore and plumb the depths of work. And to do that with great meaning and purpose.”

The onset of Covid initially kept the two of them apart, Weems in Syracuse, New York, and Triana in France. However, they worked together on Zoom, with the native of Cali, Colombia, joining in a project designed to communicate information about the terrifying onset of the pandemic that Weems organized with the Harlem artist support group, Brotherhood Sister Sol.

But once they could meet, they spent time together, exploring each other’s work and lives. “We even went to church together,” says Weems, smiling. “She believes in God and I’m Baptist, I really wanted her to see what my life is like on a Sunday, in my community.”

Triana started her career as a filmmaker but, while studying in France at Le Fresnoy studio of contemporary arts, she began to make installations that use stitching, thread and textiles to express her ideas, working through her thoughts about Colombia’s violent past and its struggle into a different future. “For me, there’s a link between the threads and the action of repair,” she explains. “I’m trying to repair the violent memory of our history and the history of my ancestors and my heritage. In a spiritual way, maybe, my work is trying to heal.”

name.value: Camila Rodríguez Triana, 2020-2022 Visual Arts PROTÉGÉÉ

title.value: For me, there’s a link between the threads and the action of repair. I’m trying to repair the violent memory of our history.

text.value:
Weems admires Triana’s way of creating. “There is something important about what it means to take your time to weave something together. I admire the way she works with her hands, it’s a slower process. To use a craft that has been associated so much with women and push it beyond those bounds is important.”

When Weems was mounting The Shape of Things – a multi-work, career-defining installation and series of performances summarizing her 40-year preoccupation with black identity and injustice – at New York’s Park Avenue Armory at the end of last year, she included Triana in meetings with designers to give her a sense of what it takes to put together such a massive project. Weems invited all 2020–2022 mentors and protégés to the event, providing the latter with a platform to present their work.

“It’s one of the ways you really learn,” she says. That practical advice extended further when she suggested Triana work with her own model-maker to mount the installation she prepared for the Rolex Arts Weekend at the Brooklyn Academy of Music (BAM) on 9-10 September. “It’s saved her tons of time. It was a real eye-opener for her.”

For Triana, this access to professional help is another example of her mentor’s generosity. But the bond between them has gone deeper. “I feel connected to her by her social interest. I think there is an important teaching for me that art is not only an aesthetic reflection, but has an important social and political commitment.”

name.value: Carrie Mae Weems , 2020-2022 Visual Arts Mentor

title.value: I admire the way she works with her hands… To use a craft that has been associated so much with women and push it beyond those bounds is important.

text.value:
The Rolex mentorship has come at a moment of great change in her life. “I was really scared at the beginning of the pandemic. But it has helped me to understand that I have to find security in myself. I have to believe I am a strong woman and have faith in myself.”

It has been of infinite value to have Weems, with her wealth of experience, to support her in this change. “When you are a strong woman, people don’t know how to deal with you,” says Weems, with a smile. “So, it’s important to let your colleagues and friends and assistants know what you need, because otherwise they will assume you’ll figure it out.”
